paper_title: Contributions to the Dart versus Arrow Debate: New Data from Holocene Projectile Points from Southeastern and Southern Brazil.

methods.description: Process for selecting archaeological sites and stemmed projectile points from southeastern and southern Brazil based on specific criteria for subsequent analysis.

steps.description: Stemmed projectile points from eight archaeological sites from southeastern and southern Brazil were selected.
steps.searchable_summary: Select stemmed projectile points from specified archaeological sites
inputs: Set of potential archaeological sites in southeastern and southern Brazil
Collection of projectile points from these sites
outputs: Initial selection of stemmed projectile points from eight sites

steps.description: The selection criteria took into account the presence of a fairly large number of projectile points in archaeological levels, the evidence for a single occupation (or at least homogeneity of material culture across archaeological levels) and the existence of radiocarbon dates for each site.
steps.searchable_summary: Filter selected sites and points based on quantity, occupation type homogeneity, and availability of radiocarbon dates
inputs: Initial selection of sites and points
Criteria: point count, occupation evidence, radiocarbon dates
outputs: Filtered set of archaeological sites and projectile points meeting selection criteria

steps.description: Table II presents the archaeological sites and their absolute and calibrated radiocarbon dates. Figure 1 presents the location of each site (see column on Table II) and a brief description is given in the supplementary material.
steps.searchable_summary: Record metadata for selected sites including location and radiocarbon dates
outputs: Structured data containing selected sites, locations, absolute and calibrated radiocarbon dates

steps.description: Because of the availability of ages, our sample is biased towards the southernmost sites (Figure 1), inside rockshelters, and located mostly in Rio Grande do Sul (RS) state. Two of the sites are located in a northern setting Tunas rockshelter in Paraná (PR) state, and Alice Boer in São Paulo (SP) state. The points recovered from Alice Boer site present shapes that are morphometrically (statistically) different from the southern sites, and therefore may represent a different cultural sphere (Okumura and Araujo 2013).
steps.searchable_summary: Document sample characteristics, geographic distribution, and potential cultural/morphometric biases
inputs: Filtered set of archaeological sites and projectile points
outputs: Documentation of sample characteristics, geographic distribution, and identified biases/differences

methods.description: Application of multiple established techniques to classify archaeological projectile points as either darts or arrows based on morphometric data and weight, exploring the hypothesis of different propulsion systems.

steps.description: We used the techniques proposed by Fenenga (1953), Bradbury (1997), Shott (1997), Hughes (1998), and Hildebrandt and King (2012) to explore the hypothesis that different propulsion systems may have been present in southern and southeastern Brazil during prehistory. The first group uses functions derived from a Discriminant Function Analysis based on ethnographic or archaeological specimens whose propulsion system was known. The other group considers a threshold value of a given linear measurement to distinguish arrowheads from dart points.
steps.searchable_summary: Identify and categorize established techniques for dart/arrow classification
inputs: List of classification techniques (Fenenga, Bradbury, Shott, Hughes, Hildebrandt & King)

steps.description: The technique proposed by Shott (1997) was based on the work of Thomas (1978)... Using four linear measurements, he calculated four pairs of equations from Discriminant Function Analysis. Such equations use four (length, shoulder width, thickness, and neck width), three (shoulder width, thickness, and neck width), two (shoulder width and thickness), or one variable (shoulder width) to estimate the proportion of darts and arrows from a given sample. Although Shott (1997) states that the equation with only one variable is the one that has better discrimination between the darts and arrows of the sample in question (85%), we chose to use all four equations due to the fact that the number of specimens that could be included in each analysis (i.e., four, three, two or one variable) is variable.
steps.searchable_summary: Apply Shott (1997) Discriminant Function Analysis equations using multiple variable sets
inputs: Projectile point measurements (length, shoulder width, thickness, neck width)
outputs: Classification results (estimated proportion of darts/arrows) for each point using Shott's method with different variable sets

steps.description: Bradbury (1997) also employed equations from Discriminant Function Analysis to discriminate arrows from darts, but used neck width and maximum width.
steps.searchable_summary: Apply Bradbury (1997) Discriminant Function Analysis equations
inputs: Projectile point measurements (neck width, maximum width)
outputs: Classification results (dart/arrow) for each point using Bradbury's method

steps.description: The techniques proposed by Fenenga (1953), by Hughes (1998)... set a threshold value of 3g: points weighing 3g or more would be considered as darts and those weighing less than 3g would be classified as arrows.
steps.searchable_summary: Classify projectile points based on a 3g weight threshold (Fenenga/Hughes method)
outputs: Classification (dart/arrow) for each point based on weight threshold

steps.description: Hildebrandt and King (2012) propose that when the sum of the neck width and maximum thickness values is greater than 11.8 mm, the projectile is classified as a dart, whereas values smaller than 11.8 mm would indicate an arrow.
steps.searchable_summary: Classify projectile points based on a threshold for the sum of neck width and maximum thickness (Hildebrandt & King method)
inputs: Projectile point measurements (neck width, maximum thickness)
outputs: Classification (dart/arrow) for each point based on sum of measurements threshold

steps.description: We used the techniques proposed by Fenenga (1953), Bradbury (1997), Shott (1997), Hughes (1998), and Hildebrandt and King (2012) to explore the hypothesis that different propulsion systems may have been present...
steps.searchable_summary: Compare and analyze classification results obtained from multiple techniques
inputs: Classification results from Fenenga (1953) / Hughes (1998) method
Classification results from Hildebrandt and King (2012) method
outputs: Comparative analysis of classification outcomes across different methods
